A manifest\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\n\n### The freedom spectrum\n\n\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\nlives:\n\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\n|---|---|\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\n\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\n\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\n   maximum depth per run.\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\n   free-text gameplay commands.\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\n   identical actions = identical run.\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\n   the validation gauntlet.\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\n\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\n\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\n\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\n  trivially empty are rejected.\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

runs/sessions-phase02-task1.jsonl:11:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_4","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' NORTH_STAR.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Generative Gaming — North Star\n\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\n\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Concept\n\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\n\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\n\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\n\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\n\n### Working fiction (revisable)\n\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \"the dungeon adapts to you\" inside\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\nfor the architecture.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\n\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\n\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\n\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\n  background while the current floor is being played.\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\n  freedom *cheap*.\n\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\n\n### Where the \"genuinely interesting\" lives\n\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\n   The Director's job description is \"be a great dungeon master,\" not \"be hard\" — surprise,\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \"Show me what the AI\n   invented for me and why\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \"dungeon diary\"\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\n\n---\n\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\n\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\n\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\n\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\n\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\n\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\nlanguage (the \"floor manifest\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\n\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\n\n### The freedom spectrum\n\n\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\nlives:\n\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\n|---|---|\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\n\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\n\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\n   maximum depth per run.\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\n   free-text gameplay commands.\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\n   identical actions = identical run.\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\n   the validation gauntlet.\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\n\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\n\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\n\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\n  trivially empty are rejected.\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\n\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\n\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\n\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\n  completionist, chaos gremlin) is fed to the Director.\n- Scored on: **validity rate** (gates 0–1), **solvability rate** (gate 2), **difficulty\n  band accuracy** (bot survival within target band), **novelty** (distance from content\n  bank and from the trace's previous floors), **responsiveness** (does the floor measurably\n  reference the trace it was given?), **latency and cost** per floor.\n- Thresholds are regression gates: a prompt \"improvement\" that drops solvability does not\n  merge.\n- Periodic agent-played full runs (an LLM player completing whole games) as an end-to-end\n  smoke of fun, not just function.\n\nA model or prompt change is treated exactly like a code change: benchmarked, compared,\naccepted on evidence.\n\n---\n\n## 6. The Player Experience (Demo Through-Line)\n\nThe five-minute experience the whole project optimizes for:\n\n1. Start a run. Floor 1 is gentle and watchful — the dungeon is learning you.\n2. Play a few floors. Notice the content bending: the items lean toward your style, an\n   enemy appears that counters your habit, narration nods at something you actually did.\n3. Meet an NPC who references your deeds and offers a quest that only makes sense for\n   *this* run.\n4. The opening narration proves it.\n7. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.started\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":null,\\\"status\\\":\\\"in_progress\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\\\n\\\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\\\n\\\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\n\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\n\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\n\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\n\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\n\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\n\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\" inside\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\n\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\n\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\n\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\n\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\n\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\" lives\\\\n\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\"\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\n\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\n\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\n\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\n\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\n\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\n\\\\n\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\nlives:\\\\n\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\n\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\n\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\n\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\n\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\n\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\\\n\\\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\\\n\\\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\\\n\\\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_8\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\n\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\n\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\n\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\n\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\n\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\n\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\" inside\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\n\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\n\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\n\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\n\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\n\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\" lives\\\\n\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\"\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\n\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\n\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\n\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\n\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\n\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\n\\\\n\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\nlives:\\\\n\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\n\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\n\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\n\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\n\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\n\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\\\n\\\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\\\n\\\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\\\n\\\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_8\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" inside\\\\\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" lives\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\\\\\nlives:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\\\\\n|---|---|\\\\\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" inside\\\\\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" lives\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\\\\\nlives:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\\\\\n|---|---|\\\\\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. System Pillars\\\\n\\\\n1. **Small legible integers.** HP in the tens, damage in single digits. Run Structure\\\\n\\\\n| Parameter | Value | Notes |\\\\n|---|---|---|\\\\n| Run depth | 12 floors [T] | Floor 12 holds the Hoard (win condition) |\\\\n| Depth bands | Shallows 1–4 · Middle 5–9 · Lowest 10–12 | Maps to WORLD §8 arc and all band tables below |\\\\n| Win | Reach the Hoard on floor 12, take one thing, run ends in `WIN` | |\\\\n| Loss | HP reaches 0 → `LOSS` | Permadeath; diary + memory persist |\\\\n| Abort | Player quits run → `ABORTED` | Recorded, remembered |\\\\n| Per-floor soft cap | 800 turns [T] | Then the Deep grows bored: escalating reinforcement spawns every 100 turns |\\\\n| Per-run hard cap | 8,000 turns [HARD] | Engine forces terminal state; finiteness invariant (NORTH_STAR §4.1) is mechanical, not hoped for |\\\\n\\\\nFloor geometry is rooms-and-corridors (classic Mystery Dungeon), generated by the\\\\nengine from manifest *parameters* — the Director chooses knobs, never tile-by-tile\\\\nlayouts:\\\\n\\\\n| Band | Grid size [T] | Rooms [T] | Layout flavors available |\\\\n|---|---|---|---|\\\\n| Shallows | 32×20 | 4–7 | open, warren |\\\\n| Middle | 40×24 | 5–9 | open, warren, halls, ring |\\\\n| Lowest | 36×22 | 3–6 | halls, ring, sanctum (single composed set piece) |\\\\n\\\\nEvery floor is guaranteed by generation + Gate 2: stairs reachable from entrance,\\\\nall quest objectives reachable, no entity placed in unreachable cells [HARD].\\\\n\\\\n## 3. Time, Turns, and Action Economy\\\\n\\\\n- Strict turn cycle: player acts → all other actors act in deterministic order\\\\n  (stable actor-id order) → end-of-turn ticks (statuses, hunger, regen).\\\\n- One action per actor per turn: move 1 tile (8-way), attack, use item, pick up,\\\\n  talk, wait, descend. No action points, no speed stats in MVP — `haste`/`slow`\\\\n  statuses grant/deny every other turn, which is the only speed mechanic.\\\\n- Tick order within end-of-turn is fixed and documented in the engine: damage-over-\\\\n  time → durations decrement → hunger → natural regen. Deterministic and testable.\\\\n\\\\n## 4. The Player Character\\\\n\\\\nState: **HP**, **Level**, **XP**, **Fullness**, inventory, equipment, statuses.\\\\nAttack and Defense are derived: base (from level) + weapon/armor + buffs.\\\\n\\\\n| Stat | Start [T] | Growth [T] | Cap [HARD] |\\\\n|---|---|---|---|\\\\n| HP | 20 | +4 per level | 99 |\\\\n| Level | 1 | XP from kills; XP to next level = 8 × current level [T] (≈3–4 band-typical kills per level) | 12 |\\\\n| Base ATK | 2 | +1 per 2 levels | — |\\\\n| Base DEF | 0 | +1 per 3 levels | — |\\\\n| Fullness | 100 | −1 per 10 turns [T]; at 0, lose 1 HP per 2 turns | 100 (200 briefly when overfed) |\\\\n\\\\n- **Natural regen:** +1 HP per 6 turns [T] while Fullness > 0 — the Shiren rhythm:\\\\n  walking is healing, hunger makes walking expensive.\\\\n- Level and everything else resets between runs. Cross-run persistence is memory\\\\n  and diary only (WORLD §7) — no meta-progression of power. **[HARD]**\\\\n- Inventory: 16 slots [T]; identical consumables stack to 5. Equipment: 1 weapon,\\\\n  1 armor, 2 charms.\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Combat Math\\\\n\\\\nOne formula, no exceptions:\\\\n\\\\n> **damage = max(1, (ATK − DEF)) , then ×(0.85–1.15) seeded variance, rounded**\\\\n\\\\n- Hit chance: flat 95% [T] for everyone (a miss is rare punctuation, not a build).\\\\n- No critical hits, no damage types, no elemental table in MVP. Damage is damage.\\\\n  (Statuses carry all the tactical flavor — a `burn` proc *is* the fire sword.)\\\\n- Ranged attacks (bolt shape) use the same formula; line of sight required.\\\\n- Every resolution logs: attacker, defender, roll context, result, in one line.\\\\n\\\\n## 6. Status Vocabulary [HARD — the closed list]\\\\n\\\\nThe complete set of statuses in the game. The Director composes freely *from* this\\\\nlist and may never invent outside it. Durations in turns; all bounds [HARD].\\\\n\\\\n| Status | Effect | Duration bound |\\\\n|---|---|---|\\\\n| `poison` | −1 HP per turn; stops at 1 HP (poison never kills) | 3–10 |\\\\n| `burn` | −2 HP per turn; can kill; cured by `cure_status` or water tiles | 2–5 |\\\\n| `regen` | +2 HP per turn | 3–10 |\\\\n| `stun` | Skip turns | 1–2 |\\\\n| `confusion` | Moves/attacks go in random direction | 2–6 |\\\\n| `slow` | Act every other turn | 3–8 |\\\\n| `haste` | Extra action every other turn | 3–8 |\\\\n| `blind` | Vision radius 1 | 3–8 |\\\\n| `shield` | +3 DEF | 3–10 |\\\\n| `weaken` | −2 ATK (min 1) | 3–8 |\\\\n\\\\nStacking: re-application refreshes duration, never stacks magnitude. `haste`+`slow`\\\\ncancel.
