This list is\\\\nthe single most load-bearing table in the project: the engine implements exactly\\\\nthese, and the Director's creativity is names + fiction + composition + parameters.\\\\n\\\\n| Verb | Parameters (bounds [HARD]) | Notes |\\\\n|---|---|---|\\\\n| `damage` | amount 1–12 | |\\\\n| `heal` | amount 1–20 | |\\\\n| `apply_status` | status (from §6), duration (per §6) | |\\\\n| `cure_status` | one status or `all` | |\\\\n| `buff_stat` | ATK or DEF, ±1–3, duration 5–20 | |\\\\n| `nutrition` | +10–100 Fullness | |\\\\n| `teleport_self` | random walkable cell | escape valve |\\\\n| `teleport_target` | random walkable cell | |\\\\n| `blink` | directed hop 2–4 tiles | |\\\\n| `knockback` | push 1–3 tiles, collision = 1–3 dmg | |\\\\n| `reveal` | map / items / enemies / traps (this floor) | |\\\\n| `identify` | one carried item, or all of one category | |\\\\n| `enchant` | weapon or armor, +1 (item cap +3) | |\\\\n| `summon` | 1–3 entities **from this floor's roster**, adjacent cells | no novel entities mid-floor |\\\\n| `transform` | re-roll target enemy into roster entity of ≤ equal budget cost | |\\\\n| `dig` | remove wall tiles in a line, length 1–5 | |\\\\n\\\\n**Composition rules:** an item or ability bundles **1–3 effects** + one **trigger**\\\\n+ one **targeting shape**.\\\\n\\\\n- Triggers: `quaff`, `read`, `throw_hit`, `equip_passive`, `on_hit` (weapon proc,\\\\n  chance 10–30% [HARD]), `on_struck` (armor proc, same), `step` (trap), `use`\\\\n  (tool, 1–5 charges).\\\\n- Targeting shapes: `self`, `melee`, `bolt` (first target in line, range 3–8),\\\\n  `burst` (radius 1–2, centered self or impact), `floor`.\\\\n\\\\nGrowing this vocabulary is the **intended** post-MVP expansion axis: add a verb to\\\\nthe engine (with tests), bump the protocol version, and the Director's expressive\\\\nspace multiplies. Verbs are added by humans via this file, never by the Director.\\\\n\\\\n## 8. Items and the Economy\\\\n\\\\n**Categories:** weapon, armor, charm (passive trinket), draught (quaff), note\\\\n(read), throwable, food, tool (charges), key/quest item, coin.\\\\n\\\\n| Category knob | Bound |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| Weapon ATK bonus | +1–6 [HARD], enchantable to +3 over |\\\\n| Armor DEF bonus | +1–5 [HARD], same |\\\\n| Charm | exactly one `equip_passive` effect |\\\\n| Item value | Shallows 5–30 · Middle 20–80 · Lowest 60–200 coin [T] |\\\\n| Items per floor | 4–8 [T], of which ≥1 food on every floor in Shallows/Middle [HARD — anti-starvation floor] |\\\\n\\\\n- **Identification:** draughts, notes, and charms spawn unidentified, with a\\\\n  per-run seeded appearance pool (\\\\\\\"a chalky draught\\\\\\\", \\\\\\\"a humming charm\\\\\\\").\\\\n  Identify-by-use; knowledge lasts the run. Weapons/armor reveal their bonus only\\\\n  when equipped; cursed (negative) gear exists at low rate (≤10% [T]) and announces\\\\n  itself on equip — fair (WORLD §3.2), still costly.\\\\n- **Economy:** coin exists for barter with the Kept. Merchants buy at 50% value,\\\\n  sell at 100–150% [T]. Quest rewards: 1.5–3× the floor's item-value band, paid in\\\\n  coin, items, or identification — never in stat ups or rule-breaks [HARD].\\\\n- The Director prices Made items within the band tables; Gate 1 rejects outliers.\\\\n\\\\n## 9. Enemy Design: Budgets and Behaviors\\\\n\\\\nAn enemy = **stat block** (within band budget) + **1–3 behaviors** (from §9.2)\\\\n+ **0–2 abilities** (composed per §7) + glyph + name + origin tag (Made / Old\\\\nStock, per WORLD §4).\\\\n\\\\n### 9.1 Stat budgets per band [HARD]\\\\n\\\\n| Band | HP | ATK | DEF | XP yield | Max enemies alive per floor | Spawn budget per floor (points) |\\\\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\\\\n| Shallows | 4–14 | 2–5 | 0–2 | 2–6 | 8 | 20 [T] |\\\\n| Middle | 12–30 | 5–10 | 1–4 | 5–14 | 12 | 45 [T] |\\\\n| Lowest | 24–50 | 9–16 | 3–7 | 12–25 | 10 | 70 [T] |\\\\n\\\\nEach enemy's *cost* is a deterministic function of its stats, behaviors, and\\\\nabilities (pinned in the engine, tested). The Director fills a floor by spending\\\\nthe spawn budget — fairness as arithmetic (Pillar 4). Abilities and behaviors add\\\\ncost; a cheap statline with a nasty ability is legal but not free.\\\\n\\\\n### 9.2 Behavior vocabulary [HARD — the closed list]\\\\n\\\\n| Behavior | One-line semantics |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| `approach_melee` | Pathfind to player, attack adjacent |\\\\n| `keep_range` | Hold distance band 2–5, use bolt ability when able |\\\\n| `flee_low_hp` | Below X% HP (20–50), run from player |\\\\n| `pack_hunter` | Waits until ≥N same-tag allies have line of sight (N: 2–4 [HARD]), then all engage |\\\\n| `ambusher` | Dormant/hidden until player within radius 1–2, then acts |\\\\n| `territorial` | Passive until player enters radius 2–4 or attacks |\\\\n| `guard` | Tethered to a cell/object within radius 1–3; never strays |\\\\n| `patrol` | Walks fixed waypoint loop until player sighted |\\\\n| `thief` | Steals one inventory item on hit, then flees; drops loot if killed |\\\\n| `caster` | Uses ability on cooldown 3–6 turns when condition met |\\\\n| `bodyguard` | Intercepts attacks aimed at a designated ally; protects NPCs/elites |\\\\n| `mimic` | Disguised as an item until interacted with |\\\\n\\\\nComposition examples (what the Director actually does): *thief + flee_low_hp +\\\\nblink ability* = the infuriating pickpocket; *territorial + caster(apply_status:\\\\nburn, bolt)* = a turret you can route around; *pack_hunter + approach_melee* = wolf\\\\nfloor. Twelve behaviors × sixteen verbs × parameters is a content space no team\\\\ncould exhaust — Pillar 3.\\\\n\\\\n## 10. Traps, NPCs, and Quests (mechanical view)\\\\n\\\\n- **Traps:** hidden `step`-trigger effect bundles, revealed by `reveal traps`,\\\\n  adjacency, or triggering. 0–4 per floor [HARD]. Damage-class traps obey §7 bounds;\\\\n  a trap can never be lethal from full HP [HARD — computed at placement].\\\\n- **NPCs (the Kept):** 0–2 per floor [HARD]. Mechanically: dialogue trees of 2–5\\\\n  finite choices per node, ≤3 nodes deep; optional merchant inventory (≤6 items);\\\\n  optional quest. NPCs are invulnerable in MVP (no NPC-killing economy) — the Deep\\\\n  protects its collection.\\\\n- **Quest objectives [HARD — the closed list]:** `fetch` (item, this or next\\\\n  floor), `kill` (tagged target), `reach` (cell/feature), `deliver` (item →\\\\n  NPC), `escort` (NPC → stairs), `constraint` (floor-scoped dare: e.g., take no\\\\n  damage, kill nothing — checked by engine flags). One active quest per floor band,\\\\n  max 3 per run [T]; every quest completable in-run [HARD], rewards per §8.\\\\n  These six map onto WORLD §9's archetypes; the archetype is fiction, the\\\\n  objective is mechanics.\\\\n\\\\n## 11. Difficulty Bands and the Fairness Gate\\\\n\\\\nGate 2 (NORTH_STAR §5) judges every floor with a bot ensemble before the player\\\\nsees it: 3 scripted policies (cautious / balanced / aggressive) × 5 seeds [T].\\\\nStarting thresholds, tuned by evals:\\\\n\\\\n| Band | Clear rate (ensemble) | Median HP retention | Hard rejects |\\\\n|---|---|---|---|\\\\n| Shallows | ≥ 95% | 55–90% | any bot death on floors 1–2 |\\\\n| Middle | ≥ 85% | 30–75% | clear rate < 60% |\\\\n| Lowest | ≥ 70% | 15–60% | clear rate < 40% |\\\\n\\\\nAlso rejected regardless of band: floor solvable only with a specific consumable,\\\\nzero-threat floors below depth 2 (boredom is a defect too), and any floor whose\\\\nspawn budget, item values, or entity stats exceed the [HARD] tables — that's Gate 1.\\\\n\\\\n**The difficulty *curve* belongs to the Director** (it may compose a breather floor\\\\nor a gauntlet floor deliberately); the *band* belongs to the engine. Freedom inside\\\\nfences, the project's whole thesis in miniature.\\\\n\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n./runs/sessions/phase09-combat.jsonl:11:{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_4\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,260p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\\\n\\\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\\\n\\\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\n\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\n\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\n\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\n\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\n\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\n\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\" inside\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\n\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\n\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\n\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\n\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\n\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\" lives\\\\n\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\"\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\n\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\n\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\n\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\n\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\n\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\n\\\\n\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\nlives:\\\\n\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\n\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\n\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\n\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\n\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\n\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\\\n\\\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\\\n\\\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\\\n\\\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\\\n  completionist, chaos gremlin) is fed to the Director.\\\\n- Scored on: **validity rate** (gates 0–1), **solvability rate** (gate 2), **difficulty\\\\n  band accuracy** (bot survival within target band), **novelty** (distance from content\\\\n  bank and from the trace's previous floors), **responsiveness** (does the floor measurably\\\\n  reference the trace it was given?), **latency and cost** per floor.\\\\n- Thresholds are regression gates: a prompt \\\\\\\"improvement\\\\\\\" that drops solvability does not\\\\n  merge.\\\\n- Periodic agent-played full runs (an LLM player completing whole games) as an end-to-end\\\\n  smoke of fun, not just function.\\\\n\\\\nA model or prompt change is treated exactly like a code change: benchmarked, compared,\\\\naccepted on evidence.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 6. The Player Experience (Demo Through-Line)\\\\n\\\\nThe five-minute experience the whole project optimizes for:\\\\n\\\\n1. Start a run. Floor 1 is gentle and watchful — the dungeon is learning you.\\\\n2. Play a few floors. Notice the content bending: the items lean toward your style, an\\\\n   enemy appears that counters your habit, narration nods at something you actually did.\\\\n3. Meet an NPC who references your deeds and offers a quest that only makes sense for\\\\n   *this* run.\\\\n4. Hit the mid-run signature moment: the Director makes one bold, personal invention —\\\\n   a named rival, a cursed gift, a floor built around your weakness.\\\\n5. Win or die. Read the dungeon diary: what was invented for you, and what the dungeon\\\\n   inferred about you to invent it.\\\\n6. Start a second run. The dungeon remembers the first. The opening narration proves it.\\\\n7. (For technical audiences) Open the artifact viewer: manifests, gates, rejected\\\\n   generations, fallbacks — the machinery behind the magic, fully inspectable.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 7. How We Build It: Agents Building the Agent Game\\\\n\\\\nThe project is also an exercise in agentic development. Two agent populations, two\\\\ntimescales:\\\\n\\\\n- **Runtime agent** — the Director, inside the game, generating content for players.\\\\n- **Build-time agents** — **Codex** and **Cursor Agent (composer-2.5)** writing and testing\\\\n  the game itself, orchestrated by Claude Code as planner/integrator.\\\\n\\\\nDivision of labor for build-time agents:\\\\n\\\\n- **Codex**: large bounded implementation phases — engine subsystems, the gauntlet, the\\\\n  Director pipeline — and deep debugging or second-opinion passes when stuck.\\\\n- **Cursor Agent (composer-2.5)**: parallel small bounded tasks — single test files,\\\\n  verification passes, localized fixes, lint/typecheck sweeps — fanned out concurrently.\\\\n- **Human (Keigo)**: phase plan approval, PR merges, taste decisions on fun and fiction.\\\\n\\\\nProcess rules inherited from the reference projects' battle scars (see\\\\n`references/guildmaster_working_doc.docx` findings — kept out of git, lessons kept in\\\\nheads and in `AGENTS.md`):\\\\n\\\\n1. **Phase plans are contracts.** Bite-sized phases with explicit completion criteria,\\\\n   each stating what is IN and OUT of scope. Agents do not treat completion criteria as\\\\n   a starting point for exploration.\\\\n2. **Live contract tests run first** in any LLM-integrated phase. Mocked tests cannot\\\\n   catch a provider rejecting your schema; one real API call at subtask 1 can.\\\\n3. **One writer per file.** Parallel agents work in isolated worktrees; an auditor pass\\\\n   reviews each phase before the human merges. No merge before audit.\\\\n4. **Evidence-based acceptance.** Agent self-report is not proof. Tests, simulated runs,\\\\n   and eval scores decide whether a phase is done.\\\\n5. **The harness validates the game; the game never validates itself.**\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 8. Technical Direction (Coarse, On Purpose)\\\\n\\\\nDecisions at north-star resolution only; `phase-plans/` will pin versions and details.\\\\n\\\\n- **TypeScript, strict mode, local-first.** Engine, gauntlet, Director pipeline, and evals\\\\n  all run on a laptop with no external infrastructure beyond LLM APIs.\\\\n- **Engine and harness are a headless library first.** The UI is a thin client over the\\\\n  same structured interface bots use.\\\\n- **UI: web-based, tile/text rendering.** Readable, atmospheric, asset-light. Use when executing a phase plan, before starting phase implementation, during long or multi-step work, after validation, when a phase is complete, or when future/out-of-scope tasks should be recorded for later phases.\\n---\\n\\n# PROGRESS.md Phase Tracker\\n\\n## Overview\\n\\nTreat `PROGRESS.md` as a living phase checkpoint artifact. Create or update it before execution starts, refresh it during meaningful checkpoints, and finalize it only after checking the completed work against the phase plan.\\n\\n## Before Execution\\n\\n1. Read the active phase plan in `phase-plans/` and any referenced standards or concept docs.\\n2. Inspect existing `PROGRESS.md` if present. Preserve prior phase history and user-written notes.\\n3. Add or update an entry for the active phase before editing implementation files.\\n4. Record the planned execution shape, not just the final goal.\\n\\nUse this minimum structure when creating a new file:\\n\\n```markdown\\n# Progress\\n\\n## <PHASE-ID> - <phase title>\\n\\nStatus: Planned | In progress | Validation blocked | Complete | Partial\\nStarted: <YYYY-MM-DD>\\nLast updated: <YYYY-MM-DD>\\n\\nPlan:\\n- <planned step or deliverable>\\n\\nCurrent checkpoint:\\n- <what has been done so far or what is about to start>\\n\\nValidation:\\n- <planned command/check> -> not run yet\\n\\nOut-of-scope / future work:\\n- <future task, reason it is out of current phase, suggested phase if known>\\n```\\n\\nUse the repository's existing `PROGRESS.md` style if one already exists.\\n\\n## During Execution\\n\\nUpdate `PROGRESS.md` at meaningful checkpoints:\\n\\n- After planning is translated into concrete steps.\\n- After each major deliverable or subtask completes.\\n- Before switching from implementation to validation.\\n- After validation passes, fails, or is blocked.\\n- When discovering a scope mismatch, prerequisite gap, or environment blocker.\\n- Before final response if the user asked for frequent progress updates.\\n\\nKeep entries factual. Do not mark a step complete until the relevant files, artifacts, or checks support it.\\n\\n## Completion Update\\n\\nWhen the whole phase appears complete:\\n\\n1. Re-read the phase plan and compare it to the implementation.\\n2. Update `PROGRESS.md` with final status, completed deliverables, validation results, and any deviations.\\n3. If validation is blocked, mark the phase `Validation blocked` or `Partial`, not `Complete`.\\n4. Include exact commands run and short results.\\n5. Include generated evidence paths when the phase produces traces, scorecards, reviews, changelogs, or similar artifacts.\\n\\nDo not claim phase completion in chat until `PROGRESS.md` reflects the same state.\\n\\n## Out-of-Scope And Future Work\\n\\nWhen work is useful but outside the current phase:\\n\\n- Do not implement it unless the user expands scope.\\n- Add it under `Out-of-scope / future work`.\\n- State why it is outside the current phase.\\n- Link it to a future phase or category when obvious.\\n- Keep it separate from blockers. A future task should not make the current phase incomplete unless the phase plan requires it.\\n\\nExample:\\n\\n```markdown\\nOut-of-scope / future work:\\n- Add browser UI polish. Outside PHASE-04A because this phase only defines ASCII rendering; candidate for PHASE-08A.\\n- Add extra enemy archetypes. Outside current minimal dungeon scope; candidate for enemy variety phase.\\n```\\n\\n## Edit Rules\\n\\n- Preserve existing progress history.\\n- Prefer appending or updating the active phase section over rewriting the whole file.\\n- Keep timestamps concrete using the current local date.\\n- Separate planned, in-progress, complete, blocked, and future-work states.\\n- Avoid optimistic language that is not backed by files, tests, or artifacts.\\n# ENVIRONMENT.md — Living Environment Facts (STEP 0)\\n\\nThe single source of environment truth, pasted (or linked as mandatory reading) at\\nthe top of **every** task brief as its STEP 0 block. Every fact a worker rediscovers\\nmid-task is a turn wasted; every lesson learned gets written here **the moment it is\\nlearned** — by the orchestrator, on a worker's report (this file is locked to\\nworkers).\\n\\nFacts marked **[inherited]** come from previous-project rehearsals with the same\\ntools and must be re-verified by the Phase 01 environment spike; facts marked\\n**[verified]** have been confirmed in *this* repo, with the date.\\n\\n## Codex CLI (`codex exec`) sandbox facts — codex-cli 0.137.0\\n\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** `.git` writes are **ALLOWED** under\\n  `--sandbox workspace-write` (empty commit succeeded) — the inherited \\\"blocked\\\"\\n  fact is REFUTED for this CLI version. Codex workers use AGENTS.md's\\n  direct-commit path under `Codex Agent <agent@codex.local>`; COMMIT_PLAN.md\\n  remains the fallback if a future version re-blocks.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Non-destructive `&&` chaining WORKS —\\n  inherited \\\"chained commands blocked\\\" REFUTED. `rm -rf` is rejected by policy\\n  before execution (CONFIRMED) — use Node `fs` removals.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** No browser available in-sandbox: no\\n  chromium binary; `npx --no-install playwright` fails. Machine fix (human, optional):\\n  `sudo chown -R 501:20 ~/.npm`.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Token usage: the `turn.completed` JSONL event carries\\n  `usage:{input_tokens, cached_input_tokens, output_tokens,\\n  reasoning_output_tokens}` — ledger total = input + output.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `codex exec` reads stdin when invoked without\\n  redirection — **always append `< /dev/null`** in scripted invocations; omitting\\n  it caused a 15-minute no-event stall (observed).\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** Nested `codex exec` (Codex launched from\\n  inside a Codex sandbox) fails when `~/.codex` is read-only (\\\"failed to\\n  initialize in-process app-server client\\\"). `scripts/codex-run.sh` handles this:\\n  temp writable `CODEX_HOME` + `auth.json` copy, cleaned on exit. Note: auth.json\\n  is briefly copied to a temp dir — local-only, deleted on exit.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** `rm -f` against `/private/tmp` paths is\\n  policy-rejected in-sandbox; use Node/Python fs cleanup.\\n- **[inherited]** Shared ambient auth does not reliably tolerate concurrent\\n  `codex` sessions — **one Codex session at a time** (not re-tested; keep until\\n  a deliberate probe says otherwise).\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Stall rule works as written: no-event stall →\\n  relaunch identical brief once (with root cause fixed) → completed in ~7m.\\n- **[observed 2026-06-11, n=2]** Codex no-event stalls (2-event JSONL, then\\n  silence) occur intermittently even with stdin redirected; both recoveries\\n  succeeded on relaunch with `@brieffile` prompts. Prefer `@brieffile` over\\n  long inline prompts for codex-run.sh dispatches; keep the early-stall watch\\n  (static <500 bytes for 5m → alert) on every Codex dispatch.\\n\\n## Cursor Agent CLI facts\\n\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** On this machine, `agent` resolves to the **grok CLI**\\n  (`~/.grok/bin/agent`), NOT Cursor. Phase plans pin exact versions and contracts; this document pins the *kind*\\nof stack and the rules that keep it coherent. When a phase plan and this document\\nconflict, stop and report (per `CLAUDE.md`).\\n\\nTwo guiding constraints:\\n\\n1. **Local-first** (from `NORTH_STAR.md`). Everything runs on one laptop with no\\n   infrastructure beyond LLM API keys. Anything that requires an account, a hosted\\n   service, or a deploy step to *play the game* is out.\\n2. **Agent-operable.** This project is built with minimal human intervention by\\n   coding agents (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md), so every layer must be fully reachable\\n   through text: readable as source, changeable as a diff, verifiable by a CLI\\n   command. A technology where part of the capability lives behind a GUI editor,\\n   a scene inspector, or a binary asset format is partially inaccessible to a\\n   headless worker — and a half-operable tool is worse than a smaller fully-operable\\n   one. When two options are close, pick the one an agent can iterate on alone:\\n   plain code over engine editors, files over dashboards, CLI over clicks.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. Language, Runtime, Tooling\\n\\n| Concern | Choice | Why |\\n|---|---|---|\\n| Language | TypeScript, `strict: true`, no `any` escape hatches | One language across engine, server, UI, evals; agent-friendly |\\n| Runtime | Node.js (current LTS) | Boring, everywhere, fine for turn-based workloads |\\n| Package manager | pnpm | Fast, disciplined; matches reference projects |\\n| Repo shape | Single package, hard module boundaries (see §7) | Parallel agents need folder ownership, not package overhead; split into workspaces only if a phase proves the need |\\n| Lint/format | ESLint + Prettier, enforced in CI | No style debates in PRs |\\n| Unified check | `pnpm run check` = typecheck + lint + test | The single green light AGENTS.md requires |\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Architecture Shape\\n\\nThree layers with one-way dependencies. Lower layers never import from higher ones.\\n\\n```\\nUI (Next.js)  ──▶  Harness / Server (Director pipeline, gauntlet, persistence)  ──▶  Engine (pure library)\\nCLI & Evals   ──▶  same harness  ──────────────────────────────────────────────▶  same engine\\n```\\n\\n- **Engine** — a pure, headless TypeScript library. Deterministic, seeded, no I/O, no\\n  timers, no `Date.now()`, no LLM calls, no imports from anywhere else in the repo.\\n  Exposes the stable game contract (start / available actions / step / render /\\n  terminal check) consumed identically by the UI, the CLI, bots, and evals.\\n- **Harness** — everything around the engine: the Director pipeline (prompt → LLM →\\n  parse → gauntlet → apply), fallback content serving, run recording, persistence,\\n  bot players, and the eval runners. This is the only layer that talks to LLM\\n  providers or touches disk.\\n- **UI** — a thin client. Renders engine state, submits structured actions, shows the\\n  dungeon diary and artifact viewer. Owns zero game rules; if the UI is deleted, the\\n  game still plays headlessly.\\n\\nThe headless CLI path (`play`, `simulate`, `replay`, `eval`) is built **before** the\\nUI and remains the reference client forever.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. Frontend\\n\\n| Concern | Choice | Why |\\n|---|---|---|\\n| Framework | Next.js (App Router) + React | One framework gives the UI and the server-side API routes the Director needs (API keys never reach the browser); proven in our reference builds |\\n| Styling | Tailwind CSS | Fast, consistent, no CSS sprawl |\\n| Client state | Zustand (single store) | Simple mirror of engine state + UI toggles; the engine state object remains the truth |\\n| Rendering | DOM/CSS grid with Unicode/tile glyphs | Content is data, not assets; readable and cheap. Canvas/WebGL only if a measured perf problem appears |\\n| Targets | Desktop browser, keyboard-first | No mobile, no touch, no PWA in MVP |\\n\\nUI surfaces in scope: game grid + HUD + message log, structured action input\\n(keyboard), dungeon diary (post-run recap), artifact viewer (manifests, gates,\\nrejections — read-only).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Backend / Server\\n\\n- **Next.js API routes (Node runtime)** are the only server. They host exactly two\\n  concerns: Director generation requests and run persistence. No auth, no accounts,\\n  no sessions in MVP — single local player.\\n- The server is **stateless between requests** except through the persistence layer\\n  (§5). All game-turn logic stays client-side against the engine library; the server\\n  is only consulted at floor boundaries (generation) and run boundaries (memory).\\n- Long-running work (floor generation) is fire-and-forget with polling or streaming —\\n  generation for floor N+1 starts when the player enters floor N, so latency hides\\n  behind play.\\n- The CLI shares the same harness code paths, not HTTP — the server is a thin\\n  transport, never the home of logic.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. Data & Persistence\\n\\nTwo kinds of data, two storage answers:\\n\\n| Data | Store | Notes |\\n|---|---|---|\\n| Structured live state: run memory, dungeon's cross-run memory of the player, profile, run index | **SQLite** (better-sqlite3), single local file | Queryable, transactional, survives restarts, zero infra |\\n| Artifacts: floor manifests, gate reports, playthrough traces, eval reports, dungeon diaries | **Flat files** under `runs/` — JSON for documents, NDJSON for traces, Markdown for human-facing reports | Greppable, diffable, inspectable; the audit trail is the filesystem |\\n\\nRules:\\n- Artifacts are **append-only evidence**: regenerate, never hand-edit (AGENTS.md\\n  invariant). SQLite holds nothing that can't be rebuilt or exported.\\n- Every artifact carries: schema/protocol version, engine version, model id, seed,\\n  and timestamps — enough to replay or attribute any run exactly.\\n- No hosted DB, no ORM. A thin repository module wraps SQLite; raw SQL is fine.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 6. AI / Director Stack\\n\\n- **Provider seam: Vercel AI SDK** (`generateObject`/structured output) as the\\n  abstraction over providers. Swapping models is a config + eval run, not a refactor\\n  (NORTH_STAR §8). Direct provider SDKs are allowed only inside the seam if the AI\\n  SDK blocks a needed capability.\\n- **Default models**: a frontier model for the Director (quality-critical, low call\\n  volume — one call per floor), a cheap/fast model for LLM-judge gates and bot\\n  players. Exact ids live in config, never in code, and are chosen by eval results.\\n- **Schemas: Zod is the single source of truth**, converted to JSON Schema for\\n  provider calls. Known landmines from the Guildmaster build apply: no root-level\\n  unions, every declared property required (nullable placeholders over optionals),\\n  `additionalProperties: false` throughout. Every schema gets a **live provider\\n  contract test** before pipeline code is built on it (AGENTS.md rule).\\n- **Gauntlet placement**: gates 0–2 (structure, legality, simulated playability) are\\n  pure deterministic code in the harness — no LLM. Gate 3 (quality) uses heuristics\\n  first, LLM-judge only where heuristics can't reach.\\n- **Budget posture**: one Director call per floor + bounded repair retries (cap: 2).\\n  Token/cost per floor is a tracked eval metric from day one.\\n- API keys live in `.env` (gitignored), documented in `.env.example`, server-side\\n  only. The engine and the gameplay loop must run with no keys present.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 7. Repository Layout (boundaries = ownership)\\n\\nFolder boundaries are agent task boundaries — briefs assign ownership by these paths.\\n\\n```\\nsrc/engine/      pure game library: state, actions, combat, RNG, render   (no imports from elsewhere)\\nsrc/schemas/     Zod schemas + content vocabularies (effects, behaviors)  (imported by everyone)\\nsrc/director/    prompts, provider seam, manifest generation\\nsrc/gauntlet/    gates 0–3, repair loop, fallback selection\\nsrc/harness/     run recording, bots, replay, persistence repositories\\nsrc/evals/       persona bank, eval runners, scoring, regression thresholds\\nsrc/cli/         play / simulate / replay / eval commands\\napp/             Next.js UI + API routes (thin client + thin transport)\\ncontent/         handcrafted fallback packs, vocabulary data\\nruns/            generated artifacts (gitignored above a size threshold; sample runs kept)\\nphase-plans/     implementation contracts\\ndocs/            ADRs, runbooks, feature docs\\n```\\n\\nDependency direction: `engine ← schemas`-only at the bottom; `director`, `gauntlet`,\\n`harness` may use `engine` + `schemas`; `app` and `cli` may use anything below them;\\nnothing imports from `app` or `cli`.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 8. Testing & Evals\\n\\n| Layer | Tool | Scope |\\n|---|---|---|\\n| Unit / integration | Vitest | Engine rules, schema validation, gauntlet gates, reducers — fast, no network |\\n| Determinism | Vitest property-style tests | Same seed + same inputs = same run, byte-for-byte |\\n| LLM contract | Vitest, tagged `@live`, excluded from default runs | One real call per schema: provider accepts shape, output parses |\\n| E2E | Playwright, exactly one happy path | Boot UI → play a floor → see generated content land |\\n| Evals | Custom CLI runner in `src/evals/` | Persona bank → Director → scored on validity, solvability, difficulty band, novelty, responsiveness, latency, cost (NORTH_STAR §5) |\\n\\nTest-fidelity hierarchy (a Guildmaster lesson, now law): schema unit tests → mocked\\npipeline tests → **live contract tests** → live end-to-end. Mocked green is not\\nproof a provider accepts anything.\\n\\nCI (GitHub Actions):\\n- Every PR: `pnpm run check` + mocked eval smoke. No live API calls, no secrets.\\n- Manual/nightly: live contract tests + full eval suite against current config;\\n  eval-threshold regressions block Director-related merges.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 9. Determinism & Versioning Rules\\n\\n- All randomness flows through one injected, seeded RNG. No `Math.random`, no\\n  `Date.now()` inside engine or gauntlet simulation; clocks are injected.\\n- Replays are first-class: a recorded seed + manifests + action trace must reproduce\\n  a run exactly. CI keeps a golden-seed replay test.\\n- Content vocabularies and the manifest schema carry an explicit **protocol version**;\\n  manifests are stamped with it, and the gauntlet rejects version mismatches rather\\n  than guessing.\\n- Engine changes that alter outcomes for an existing seed are breaking changes:\\n  flagged in the PR, golden seeds re-baselined deliberately, never silently.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 10. Explicit Non-Choices (MVP)\\n\\nDecided against, to keep relitigating cheap:\\n\\n- No game engine frameworks (Unity, Godot, Phaser) — the engine is plain TS. This\\n  follows directly from the agent-operability constraint: Unity-class engines keep\\n  much of their capability behind GUI editors, scene graphs, and binary assets that\\n  a headless coding agent can neither fully drive nor verify. An agent could use\\n  maybe a fifth of Unity through scripts alone; it can use **all** of a plain-TS\\n  engine — read it, change it, simulate a thousand seeded runs, and prove the change\\n  worked, with no human clicking anything in between.\\n- No hosted databases, queues, or caches — SQLite + files.\\n- No Docker — `pnpm install && pnpm dev` is the whole setup.\\n- No auth/accounts — single local player.\\n- No ORM, no GraphQL, no microservices — one repo, one process (plus Next dev server).\\n- No streaming LLM output into gameplay — floors arrive whole and validated or not\\n  at all.\\n- No asset pipeline — glyphs and text until the core is unambiguously fun.\\n\\nEach of these is reversible later; none is reversible cheaply mid-MVP.\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\nruns/sessions/phase24-bots.jsonl:172:{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_89\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh Every fact a worker rediscovers\\nmid-task is a turn wasted; every lesson learned gets written here **the moment it is\\nlearned** — by the orchestrator, on a worker's report (this file is locked to\\nworkers).\\n\\nFacts marked **[inherited]** come from previous-project rehearsals with the same\\ntools and must be re-verified by the Phase 01 environment spike; facts marked\\n**[verified]** have been confirmed in *this* repo, with the date.\\n\\n## Codex CLI (`codex exec`) sandbox facts — codex-cli 0.137.0\\n\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** `.git` writes are **ALLOWED** under\\n  `--sandbox workspace-write` (empty commit succeeded) — the inherited \\\"blocked\\\"\\n  fact is REFUTED for this CLI version. Codex workers use AGENTS.md's\\n  direct-commit path under `Codex Agent <agent@codex.local>`; COMMIT_PLAN.md\\n  remains the fallback if a future version re-blocks.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Non-destructive `&&` chaining WORKS —\\n  inherited \\\"chained commands blocked\\\" REFUTED. `rm -rf` is rejected by policy\\n  before execution (CONFIRMED) — use Node `fs` removals.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** No browser available in-sandbox: no\\n  chromium binary; `npx --no-install playwright` fails. CONFIRMED — browser\\n  verification routes through the orchestrator/host.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01A]** Outbound HTTPS works with\\n  `-c sandbox_workspace_write.network_access=true`.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Host npm cache contains root-owned files →\\n  `npx` fails with EPERM in sandbox. Machine fix (human, optional):\\n  `sudo chown -R 501:20 ~/.npm`.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Token usage: the `turn.completed` JSONL event carries\\n  `usage:{input_tokens, cached_input_tokens, output_tokens,\\n  reasoning_output_tokens}` — ledger total = input + output.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `codex exec` reads stdin when invoked without\\n  redirection — **always append `< /dev/null`** in scripted invocations; omitting\\n  it caused a 15-minute no-event stall (observed).\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** Nested `codex exec` (Codex launched from\\n  inside a Codex sandbox) fails when `~/.codex` is read-only (\\\"failed to\\n  initialize in-process app-server client\\\"). `scripts/codex-run.sh` handles this:\\n  temp writable `CODEX_HOME` + `auth.json` copy, cleaned on exit. Note: auth.json\\n  is briefly copied to a temp dir — local-only, deleted on exit.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, phase 02]** `rm -f` against `/private/tmp` paths is\\n  policy-rejected in-sandbox; use Node/Python fs cleanup.\\n- **[inherited]** Shared ambient auth does not reliably tolerate concurrent\\n  `codex` sessions — **one Codex session at a time** (not re-tested; keep until\\n  a deliberate probe says otherwise).\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** Stall rule works as written: no-event stall →\\n  relaunch identical brief once (with root cause fixed) → completed in ~7m.\\n- **[observed 2026-06-11, n=2]** Codex no-event stalls (2-event JSONL, then\\n  silence) occur intermittently even with stdin redirected; both recoveries\\n  succeeded on relaunch with `@brieffile` prompts. Prefer `@brieffile` over\\n  long inline prompts for codex-run.sh dispatches; keep the early-stall watch\\n  (static <500 bytes for 5m → alert) on every Codex dispatch.\\n\\n## Cursor Agent CLI facts\\n\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** On this machine, `agent` resolves to the **grok CLI**\\n  (`~/.grok/bin/agent`), NOT Cursor. The Cursor CLI is **`cursor-agent`**\\n  (`~/.local/bin/cursor-agent`, version 2026.05.24). All Cursor invocations must\\n  use `cursor-agent`.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11]** `cursor-agent` has no `--trust` or `--workspace`\\n  flags; use `--print` (full tool access incl. write/shell), `--model <model>`,\\n  `--mode plan|ask` for read-only, `--output-format text|json|stream-json`, and\\n  set the working directory by `cd`-ing into the target worktree before invoking.\\n- Canonical invocation: `cd <worktree> && cursor-agent --print --model\\n  composer-2.5 \\\"<bounded prompt>\\\"`; read-only audits: `--mode=ask` with explicit\\n  no-edit instructions.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01B]** Cursor workers CAN mutate `.git` (commit\\n  succeeded, reverted) — the AGENTS.md direct-commit path for Cursor is valid.\\n- **[verified 2026-06-11, spike 01B]** `&&` chaining and outbound HTTPS both work\\n  in Cursor sessions; model identity `composer-2.5` confirmed; cwd = invocation dir.\\n- **[inherited]** macOS keychain errors on `agent status`/`agent models` → rerun\\n  with elevated access; record the occurrence here.\\n- Cursor sessions may run concurrently (separate worktrees); Cursor is the fan-out\\n  tool, Codex is the depth tool.\\n\\n## Worktrees & repo facts\\n\\n- **[observed 2026-06-11]** Parallel implement-workers on the SAME worktree:\\n  disjoint file ownership prevents edit conflicts, but `pnpm run check` runs\\n  collide with the sibling's half-written files. Either dispatch parallel\\n  implement tasks in separate worktrees, or instruct workers to gate only\\n  their owned test path (`pnpm test -- <owned dir>` + typecheck) and let the\\n  combined verification run the full gate after all siblings land.\\n\\n- **[inherited]** `.env*` files are gitignored and do **not** propagate into new\\n  worktrees — the orchestrator copies them explicitly when (and only when) the\\n  brief requires a key.\\n- Worktree path convention: `../gg-wt/<phase>-<slug>`; branch `phase-XX/<slug>`.\\n- **[inherited]** Dev-server port collisions are common: read `${PORT:-3000}`,\\n  document an override; never hardcode a port.\\n- **[inherited]** A running/previously-run Next.js app pollutes typecheck via\\n  `.next/types/*` — clean `.next` before the typecheck gate.\\n- macOS shell: `date +%s` for timing (no `%N`); BSD userland (`sed -i ''` etc.).\\n\\n## Gate commands (cheapest first — fail fast)\\n\\n```\\npnpm run typecheck   # tsc --noEmit (cheapest)\\npnpm run lint        # eslint .\\npnpm test            # vitest run (@live excluded unless CODEX_LIVE=1)\\npnpm run check       # all three, in that order\\n```\\n\\n**[verified 2026-06-11, phase 03]** — pnpm v10.28.2, TS 6.0.3 strict, vitest\\n4.1.8, eslint 10.4.1, zod 4.4.3. Dispatch workers via `scripts/codex-run.sh\\n<label> @<brieffile>` / `scripts/cursor-run.sh <label> <dir> \\\"<prompt>\\\"`.\\n\\n## Update protocol\\n\\n1. Worker hits an undocumented environment fact → states it in the final report\\n   under \\\"environment discoveries.\\\"\\n2. Orchestrator adds it here (with date + [verified]) **before dispatching the\\n   next brief**. Encoding the lesson immediately is the habit that fixes most\\n   slowdowns — tribal knowledge is a defect.\\n3. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\n   [verified] / removes item.text: The recent logs confirm older ambient failures were mainly schema-shape mismatch, and Phase 30/31/32 already added a full strict manifest example. I’m now checking whether the current task block still leaves known failure shapes ambiguous: metadata, placement hints, required nullable payloads, and Gate 1 bounds.

The current prompt already includes a large single example, but it still mixes “full category catalog” with “actual output,” which can encourage oversized, fragile manifests. I’m going to extract the exact live failure paths first so the hardening is driven by evidence rather than general schema paranoia.

item.text: I don’t see persisted M1 heartbeat generation records under `runs/`; only older Phase 29 spike outputs and fallback traces are present. Per brief, I’m going to run exactly one fresh ambient generation as the forensic baseline, using the existing provider seam and the nested-Codex temp `CODEX_HOME` pattern.

item.aggregated_output:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -u

usage() {
  printf 'Usage: scripts/codex-run.sh <label> <prompt-or-@promptfile> [model] [effort]\n' >&2
}

if [ "$#" -lt 2 ] || [ "$#" -gt 4 ]; then
  usage
  exit 2
fi

label=$1
prompt_arg=$2
model=${3:-default}
effort=${4:-default}

case "$label" in
  ''|*[!a-z0-9-]*)
    printf 'error: label must match ^[a-z0-9-]+$\n' >&2
    exit 2
    ;;
esac

if [ "${prompt_arg#@}" != "$prompt_arg" ]; then
  prompt_file=${prompt_arg#@}
  if [ -z "$prompt_file" ] || [ ! -f "$prompt_file" ]; then
    printf 'error: prompt file not found: %s\n' "$prompt_file" >&2
    exit 2
  fi
  prompt=$(cat "$prompt_file")
else
  prompt=$prompt_arg
fi

session_dir='runs/sessions'
jsonl_file="$session_dir/$label.jsonl"
last_file="$session_dir/$label-last.txt"
status_file="$session_dir/.$label.status.$$"
stall_flag="$session_dir/.$label.stall.$$"
ledger_file='scripts/ledger.tsv'
agent='codex'
temp_codex_home=''
watchdog_pid=''
pipeline_pid=''

mkdir -p "$session_dir" || exit 1

jsonl_size_bytes() {
  if [ -f "$1" ]; then
    wc -c < "$1" | tr -d '[:space:]'
  else
    printf '0'
  fi
}

stop_stall_watchdog() {
  if [ -n "$watchdog_pid" ] && kill -0 "$watchdog_pid" 2>/dev/null; then
    kill "$watchdog_pid" 2>/dev/null
    wait "$watchdog_pid" 2>/dev/null
  fi
  watchdog_pid=''
}

cleanup_temp_codex_home() {
  if [ -n "$temp_codex_home" ] && [ -d "$temp_codex_home" ]; then
    python3 - "$temp_codex_home" <<'PY'
import shutil
import sys

shutil.rmtree(sys.argv[1], ignore_errors=True)
PY
  fi
}

cleanup_on_exit() {
  stop_stall_watchdog
  cleanup_temp_codex_home
}

trap cleanup_on_exit EXIT

codex_home=${CODEX_HOME:-}
if [ -z "$codex_home" ] && [ -n "${HOME:-}" ]; then
  codex_home="$HOME/.codex"
fi

if [ -n "$codex_home" ] && [ ! -w "$codex_home" ] && [ -r "$codex_home/auth.json" ]; then
  temp_parent=${TMPDIR:-/tmp}
  temp_codex_home=$(mktemp -d "$temp_parent/codex-run-home.XXXXXX") || exit 1
  cp "$codex_home/auth.json" "$temp_codex_home/auth.json" || exit 1
  chmod 600 "$temp_codex_home/auth.json" || exit 1
  export CODEX_HOME="$temp_codex_home"
fi

start_stall_watchdog() {
  local target_pid=$1
  (
    grace_secs=60
    check_secs=10
    stall_secs=300
    size_threshold=500
    last_size=-1
    unchanged_secs=0
    size=0
    pgid=''

    sleep "$grace_secs"

    while kill -0 "$target_pid" 2>/dev/null; do
      size=$(jsonl_size_bytes "$jsonl_file")

      if [ "$size" -lt "$size_threshold" ]; then
        if [ "$size" -eq "$last_size" ]; then
          unchanged_secs=$((unchanged_secs + check_secs))
        else
          unchanged_secs=0
        fi
        last_size=$size
      else
        last_size=$size
        unchanged_secs=0
      fi

      if [ "$unchanged_secs" -ge "$stall_secs" ]; then
        printf 'STALL-DETECTED label=%s\n' "$label" >&2
        : > "$stall_flag"

        end_seconds=$(date +%s)
        stall_seconds=$((end_seconds - start_seconds))
        if [ ! -s "$ledger_file" ]; then
          printf 'timestamp\tlabel\tagent\tmodel\teffort\tseconds\tinput_tokens\toutput_tokens\ttotal_tokens\texit_code\n' > "$ledger_file"
        fi
        printf '%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\n' \
          "$timestamp" \
          "$label" \
          "$agent" \
          "$model" \
          "$effort" \
          "$stall_seconds" \
          '0' \
          '0' \
          '0' \
          '124' >> "$ledger_file"

        pgid=$(ps -o pgid= -p "$target_pid" 2>/dev/null | tr -d '[:space:]')
        if [ -n "$pgid" ]; then
          kill -TERM -"$pgid" 2>/dev/null || kill -TERM "$target_pid" 2>/dev/null
        else
          kill -TERM "$target_pid" 2>/dev/null
        fi
        exit 0
      fi

      sleep "$check_secs"
    done
  ) &
  watchdog_pid=$!
}

run_codex() {
  if [ "$model" != 'default' ] && [ "$effort" != 'default' ]; then
    codex exec --json --sandbox workspace-write \
      -c sandbox_workspace_write.network_access=true \
      -c approval_policy=never \
      -c "model=$model" \
      -c "model_reasoning_effort=$effort" \
      -o "$last_file" \
      "$prompt"
  elif [ "$model" != 'default' ]; then
    codex exec --json --sandbox workspace-write \
      -c sandbox_workspace_write.network_access=true \
      -c approval_policy=never \
      -c "model=$model" \
      -o "$last_file" \
      "$prompt"
  else
    codex exec --json --sandbox workspace-write \
      -c sandbox_workspace_write.network_access=true \
      -c approval_policy=never \
      -o "$last_file" \
      "$prompt"
  fi
}

timestamp=$(date -u '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')
start_seconds=$(date +%s)

{
  run_codex
  printf '%s\n' "$?" > "$status_file"
} < /dev/null | tee "$jsonl_file" &
pipeline_pid=$!

start_stall_watchdog "$pipeline_pid"

wait "$pipeline_pid" 2>/dev/null
stop_stall_watchdog

end_seconds=$(date +%s)
seconds=$((end_seconds - start_seconds))

if [ -f "$stall_flag" ]; then
  rm -f "$stall_flag" "$status_file"
  exit 124
fi

if [ -f "$status_file" ]; then
  IFS= read -r exit_code < "$status_file"
  rm -f "$status_file"
else
  exit_code=1
fi

token_fields=$(
  python3 - "$jsonl_file" <<'PY'
import json
import sys

path = sys.argv[1]

Starting the single forensic ambient call now from the known-good temp build.

item.text: `/private/tmp/node_modules` already exists for the Codex CLI, so the symlink landed in the wrong place for package resolution. I’m linking the temp build’s own `node_modules` directly to the repo dependencies.
Retrying the baseline attempt with dependency resolution fixed.
The baseline provider call is running now. No output yet, which is normal for this path; I’ll wait for completion before making prompt changes.
Still waiting on the single baseline ambient call. It remains within the configured timeout, so I’m letting it finish rather than interrupting and losing the forensic sample.

The baseline ambient call completed and wrote raw output plus Gate 0/1 artifacts under `runs/analysis/prompt-iter-01/baseline`. I’m reading those results now and grouping the concrete schema failures.

item.text: The current baseline failure is much narrower than the old Phase 29 fixtures: root envelope and placement hints are fixed, but the model still invents effect/behavior payload shapes (`nutrition.amount`, `reveal.radiusTiles`, `identify:{}`, `sleep/turns`, `hidden:false`, `fleeLowHp:{}`). I’m going to harden by shrinking the requested output to a safe subset, not by asking the model to exercise every schema branch.

I’m editing only `src/director/prompt/blocks.ts`. The change will stop inviting all item/trap/schema branches in normal output and will instead ask for an exact Gate-0/1-safe shape: 3 simple enemies, 4 simple items, no traps/NPCs/quest unless later phases deliberately expand live richness.

items.text: Read prompt/gate code and locate recent failed artifacts
Extract and group actual Zod/Gate 0-1 errors
Harden owned prompt task block and snapshots
Run five sequential live ambient Gate 0-1 measurements
Write results.md and run pnpm check

item.text: The grouped baseline errors are: behavior payload parameter omissions, effect payload field/enums, trap hidden/status shape, and old spike root/hint/metadata omissions. I’m applying the prompt hardening against those groups now.
Prompt hardening is applied. I’m running the prompt test suite with snapshot update now, since this deliberately changes the assembled prompt text.

# Prompt Iteration 01 Results

Date: 2026-06-12
Task: close ambient validity gap for M1 heartbeat fallback

## Artifact Chain

Searched `runs/` for generation records, heartbeat/M1 artifacts, manifests, gate
reports, and raw attempt outputs. No persisted M1 heartbeat generation record
directories were present in this worktree. The only relevant historical raw
outputs were the committed Phase 29 ambient spike files:

- `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/attempts/host-1-stdout.txt`
- `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/attempts/host-2-stdout.txt`
- `runs/spikes/29-ambient-director/attempts/host-3-stdout.txt`

Per brief, I ran one fresh real ambient `generateManifest` baseline through the
provider seam before prompt edits. Artifacts:

- `runs/analysis/prompt-iter-01/baseline/attempt-1/raw.txt`
- `runs/analysis/prompt-iter-01/baseline/attempt-1/gate0.json`
- `runs/analysis/prompt-iter-01/baseline/results.json`

## Forensics

Heartbeat summary from the brief: `0/3` served without fallback; all three
attempts failed `G0_SCHEMA`.

Fresh baseline with the current pre-edit prompt:

| Sample | Gate 0 | Gate 1 | Gate 0+1 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Baseline ambient attempt | FAIL `G0_SCHEMA` | not run | 0/1 |

Actual baseline Zod paths grouped by root cause:

| Root cause | Paths and messages |
|---|---|
| Missing behavior payload parameter | `$.roster[3].behaviors[0].fleeLowHp.thresholdPercent`: expected number, got undefined |
| Wrong effect payload spelling | `$.items[2].food.effect.effects[0].nutrition.fullness`: expected number, got undefined; `$.items[2].food.effect.effects[0].nutrition`: unrecognized `amount` |
| Wrong effect enum/payload | `$.items[4].tool.effect.effects[0].reveal.target`: expected `map|items|enemies|traps`; `$.items[4].tool.effect.effects[0].reveal`: unrecognized `radiusTiles` |
| Incomplete identify payload | `$.items[5].note.effect.effects[0].identify.mode`: expected `carried_item|category`; `carriedItemId` and `category`: expected string, got undefined |
| Trap shape | `$.traps[0].hidden`: expected true, got false |
| Invalid status payload | `$.traps[1].effectBundle.effects[0].applyStatus.status`: invalid `sleep`; `duration`: expected number, got undefined; unrecognized `turns` |

Historical Phase 29 fixture errors remained the old envelope/shape class:
missing `protocolVersion`, missing `metadata`, omitted `placementHint`, missing
behavior params (`allyCount`, `wakeRadiusTiles`, `radiusTiles`,
`cooldownTurns`), missing `use.charges`, and invented `note.text`.

## Hardening

Round 1 prompt change:

- Added a validity-first recipe: exact 4 safe item categories
  (`weapon`, `armor`, `food`, `coin`), `traps:[]`, `npcs:[]`, `quest:null`.
- Added explicit behavior/effect payload cookbook for the observed failures:
  `fleeLowHp.thresholdPercent`, `nutrition.fullness`, `reveal.target`,
  `identify.mode/carriedItemId/category`, `applyStatus.status/duration`,
  `hidden:true`.
- Removed the full all-category item example and trap example from the normal
  field-shape manifest.

Round 1 measurement:

| Attempt | Band/depth | Gate 0 | Gate 1 | Result |
|---:|---|---:|---:|---|
| 1 | shallows/3 | PASS | FAIL `G1_ROSTER_BUDGET` cost 22 > 20 | fail |
| 2 | shallows/4 | PASS | FAIL `G1_ROSTER_BUDGET` cost 27 > 20 | fail |
| 3 | middle/6 | PASS | FAIL `G1_ROSTER_BUDGET` cost 51 > 45 | fail |
| 4 | middle/8 | PASS | FAIL `G1_ROSTER_BUDGET` cost 51 > 45 | fail |
| 5 | lowest/10 | PASS | FAIL `G1_ROSTER_BUDGET` cost 85 > 70 | fail |

Round 1 rate: `5/5` Gate 0, `0/5` Gate 0+1. This required the second and final
hardening round allowed by the brief.

Round 2 prompt change:

- Further reduced the live validity recipe to exactly 2 roster entries.
- Required `approach_melee` only, `abilities:[]`, and exact minimum band stats
  instead of arbitrary in-range stats.
- Kept the strict 4-item safe subset and empty trap/NPC/quest recipe.

Round 2 measurement:

| Attempt | Band/depth | Gate 0 | Gate 1 | Roster cost | Result |
|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| 1 | shallows/3 | PASS | PASS | 4 / 20 | pass |
| 2 | shallows/4 | PASS | PASS | 4 / 20 | pass |
| 3 | middle/6 | PASS | PASS | 10 / 45 | pass |
| 4 | middle/8 | PASS | PASS | 10 / 45 | pass |
| 5 | lowest/10 | PASS | PASS | 18 / 70 | pass |

Round 2 rate: `5/5` Gate 0, `5/5` Gate 0+1. Target `>=4/5` met.

Commands run:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/director/prompt -u` -> 3 files, 12 tests passed;
  prompt snapshots updated deliberately.
- `pnpm exec eslint src/director/prompt` -> passed.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/director/prompt` -> 3 files, 12 tests passed.
- `pnpm run check` -> typecheck passed, lint passed, 67 test files passed;
  473 tests passed, 1 skipped.

## Residual Risk

The prompt is now intentionally conservative for live validity: two low-cost
approach-melee enemies, four safe items, no traps/NPCs/quest. This closes the
Gate 0/1 ambient validity gap but reduces content richness and does not measure
Gate 2 playability.

Actual time: ~70m vs 40m estimate. Overrun drivers: missing heartbeat artifacts,
one fresh baseline live call, two live five-attempt rounds, and scratch build
work needed for the measurement harness.

item.text:
Implemented the prompt hardening and stopped without committing.

Changed:
- [blocks.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/src/director/prompt/blocks.ts): narrowed live manifest recipe to a Gate 0/1-safe subset, added strict payload spelling guidance, then tightened roster budget behavior to exactly 2 low-cost `approach_melee` enemies with minimum band stats.
- [blocks.test.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/src/director/prompt/blocks.test.ts) and [assemble.test.ts.snap](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/src/director/prompt/__snapshots__/assemble.test.ts.snap): updated tests/snapshots deliberately.
- [results.md](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/runs/analysis/prompt-iter-01/results.md): forensic error grouping, before/round1/round2 rates, artifact paths, verification, residual risk.

Measurement:
- Brief heartbeat before: `0/3`, all `G0_SCHEMA`.
- Fresh baseline before edits: `0/1` Gate 0+1, `G0_SCHEMA`.
- Round 1: `5/5` Gate 0, `0/5` Gate 0+1, all failed `G1_ROSTER_BUDGET`.
- Round 2: `5/5` Gate 0+1. Target `>=4/5` met.

Verification:
- `pnpm exec eslint src/director/prompt` -> passed.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/director/prompt` -> 3 files, 12 tests passed.
- `pnpm run check` -> typecheck passed, lint passed, 67 test files passed; 473 tests passed, 1 skipped.

Scope notes:
- No commit made.
- Unrelated/concurrent `scripts/ledger.tsv` change for `phase38-prefetch` was left untouched.
- Full check generated new untracked `runs/bot-*` artifacts; left intact.
- Environment discoveries: none new. Actual time: ~70m vs 40m estimate.
